data.title: Mirion Technologies Up Over 13%, on Track for Largest Percent Increase Since November 2023 — Data Talk
data.description:
Mirion Technologies, Inc. Class A (MIR) is currently at $24.34, up $2.91 or 13.56%

  • On pace for largest percent increase since Nov. 2, 2023, when it rose 14.2%
  • Snaps a two day losing streak
  • Down 0.43% this week; worst weekly performance since the week ending Sept. 12, 2025, when it fell 0.74%
  • Up 18.71% month-to-date; on pace for best month since May 2025, when it rose 21.04%
  • Up 39.46% year-to-date
  • Down 1.08% from its all-time closing high of $24.60 on Sept. 23, 2025
  • Up 120.23% from 52 weeks ago (Sept. 27, 2024), when it closed at $11.05
  • Down 1.08% from its 52-week closing high of $24.60 on Sept. 23, 2025
  • Up 122.44% from its 52-week closing low of $10.94 on Oct. 1, 2024
  • Traded as high as $24.34
  • Up 13.58% at today's intraday high; largest intraday percent increase since Nov. 2, 2023, when it rose as much as 14.35%

All data as of 12:55:16 PM ET

Source: Dow Jones Market Data, FactSet

data.title: Anthropic to triple international workforce as AI models drive growth outside US
data.description:
Anthropic said on Friday it plans to triple its international workforce and expand its applied artificial intelligence team by fivefold this year to meet a surge in demand for its Claude AI models outside the United States.

Nearly 80% of consumer usage for Claude comes from outside the United States, with per-person usage in countries like South Korea, Australia and Singapore outpacing that of America, the company said.

Anthropic, valued at $183 billion and backed by Google-parent Alphabet and Amazon.com , has distinguished itself, in part, by building AI models that excel at coding.

Its Claude large language models are widely regarded as one of the most powerful frontier models on the market, and this has helped grow the company's global business customer base from under 1,000 to more than 300,000 in two years.

The company's run-rate revenue had grown to more than $5 billion by August from about $1 billion at the beginning of the year.

As international demand drives the company's momentum, Anthropic plans to hire for more than 100 new positions across Dublin, London and Zurich.

The company also plans to open its first Asian office in Tokyo, besides additional office locations in Europe.

The global expansion is led by Chris Ciauri, who recently joined as managing director of International following Paul Smith's appointment as chief commercial officer.

"The global demand for Claude is extraordinary—from financial services in London to manufacturing in Tokyo, enterprises are trusting Claude to power their mission-critical operations," Ciauri said.

Earlier this week, Microsoft signed a deal with Anthropic to integrate Claude models into its Copilot assistant, marking a shift for the generative AI chatbot that has primarily used OpenAI.

data.title: Polish president signs bill freezing energy prices in fourth quarter
data.description:
Polish President Karol Nawrocki signed a bill extending the freezing of energy prices for households into the fourth quarter, an aide said on Friday.

The National Bank of Poland has repeatedly pointed to the measure, initially aimed at protecting household budgets from a spike in energy prices following Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine, as a key factor determining consumer price inflation.

Nawrocki also signed a bill extending social benefits for Ukrainians by six months, but tightened the eligibility requirements, the chief of the president's chancellery Zbigniew Bogucki said. He added this was the last time the president would extend the benefits.

data.title: Nigeria court orders separatist Kanu to face trial, rejects bid to dismiss charges
data.description:
By Camillus Eboh

A Nigerian court on Friday rejected a bid by separatist leader Nnamdi Kanu to be freed, ruling that prosecutors had presented enough evidence for him to answer terrorism and treason charges.

Kanu, a British citizen and leader of the banned Indigenous People of Biafra (IPOB), was first arrested in Nigeria in 2015, skipped bail in 2017, and was rearrested in Kenya in 2021 before being charged with seven counts of terrorism in Nigeria. He has pleaded not guilty to the charges, which carry life sentences.

Judge James Omotosho dismissed Kanu's "no-case submission", saying the Department of State Services (DSS), which is prosecuting on behalf of the government, had established a case against him.

"It would be in the best interests of Kanu to be given the opportunity to clear some issues raised against him in the terrorism charges," Omotosho said.

Kanu, leader of IPOB, has been in DSS custody since his extradition in 2021.

The court also ordered Nigeria's medical association to set up an eight-member panel to assess Kanu's health and advise on his request to be moved from DSS detention to the state-run National Hospital. His lawyer cited a report recommending urgent transfer and possible treatment abroad.

But Prosecutor Adegboyega Awomolo opposed the move, citing security risks and insisting DSS had complied with all court orders on Kanu's welfare.

The case was adjourned to October 8 for the medical panel's report.

data.title: KB Home Could Have Bumpy Path Back to Build-to-Order Homes — Market Talk
data.description: KB Home may face obstacles in its path back to build-to-order homes, say Zelman & Associates analysts. Right now, there is an abundance of spec inventory on the market and KB Home's competitors are offering aggressive incentives to offload it. That may make it difficult for the company to compete using its build-to-order offerings, especially as it has adopted the strategy of cutting price rather than offering incentives. "It remains to be seen if KBH is able to differentiate itself as a build-to-order builder focusing on transparent pricing rather than an incentive-heavy pricing approach," the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

26 September 2025

Further to the FTSE Russell notice published on 27 August 2025 and following publication of the stock terms and election results in relation to the voluntary liquidation of Henderson European Trust (UK, constituent) and Scheme of Reconstruction with Fidelity European Trust (UK, constituent), please see details of affected indexes and effective dates below:

Fidelity European Trust (UK, BK1PKQ9) will remain in the FTSE 250 Index.

data.title: Fed's Bowman says decisive rate cuts needed to offset labor market risks
data.description:
Key points:
  • Bowman urges proactive rate cuts to address labor market fragility
  • Fed's recent rate cut aimed at bolstering job market amid inflation concerns
  • Bowman supports smaller balance sheet, tweaks to Standing Repo Facility

By Michael S. Derby

Federal Reserve Vice-chair for Supervision Michelle Bowman reiterated Friday that she believes decisive interest rate cuts are needed to ward off rising trouble in the job market.

“Recent data show a materially more fragile labor market along with inflation that, excluding tariffs, has continued to hover not far above our target,” Bowman said in the text of a speech to be delivered before a gathering of the Forecasters Club of New York.

Citing many months’ worth of data showing mounting issues in the job market, Bowman said it is time for the rate-setting Federal Open Market Committee “to act decisively and proactively to address decreasing labor market dynamism and emerging signs of fragility.”

She said “we are at serious risk of already being behind the curve in addressing deteriorating labor market conditions,” and noted “should these conditions continue, I am concerned that we will need to adjust policy at a faster pace and to a larger degree going forward.”

When the rate setting Federal Open Market Committee met last month policymakers trimmed their overnight interest rate target range by a quarter of a percentage point to between 4% and 4.25%. While officials are still worried about inflation being above their target, their rate cut was aimed at helping bolster the job market.

One Fed governor voted in favor of a larger rate cut but Bowman joined with her colleagues to support the 25-basis point easing. Bowman had dissented at the Fed’s meeting at the end of July in favor of a rate cut while most officials then favored keeping rates steady.

In her speech, Bowman brushed off fears that President Donald Trump’s trade tariffs are on track to create a persistent inflation problem. She said when tariffs are taken out of the equation, price pressures have “continued to hover not far above our target.”

And although inflation is still over the 2% target, Fed policy should “focus on the side of the mandate that is showing signs of deterioration or fragility,” and right now, which means acting to support the job market, the official said.

Bowman also took on the state of the Fed’s ongoing contraction of its bond holdings and said “my preference is to maintain the smallest balance sheet possible with reserve balances at a level closer to scarce than ample.”

Shrinking the balance sheet as much as possible now gives the Fed flexibility to respond to future troubles if needed, the official said. Bowman also noted she’d like an all Treasury balance sheet tilted toward shorter-dated holdings, and noted the Fed, if needed, could rejigger its holdings toward longer-date bonds if needed, without increasing the overall size of its holdings.

Bowman also addressed Fed liquidity facilities in her remarks and expressed interest in changes to the Standing Repo Facility, or SRF, a new and largely untested tool the Fed created in 2021 to provide a shock absorber for markets when liquidity ran short. The SRF is expected to see major usage next week when market participants manage liquidity needs over the quarter-end.

“My preference would be for a minimum bid rate higher than the top of the federal funds rate target range in order to emphasize that the SRF’s purpose is to serve only as a backstop,” rather than a normal source of funding, Bowman said. “A rate above the top of the target range would be more likely to discourage use of the facility outside of exceptional market-wide episodes of acute stress.”

data.title: Dow Jones Top Markets Headlines at 1 PM ET: Inflation rises again, PCE shows, but not enough to alarm the Fed | Stocks ...
data.description:
Inflation rises again, PCE shows, but not enough to alarm the Fed

A key measure of inflation rose at a slightly elevated pace in August, but not enough to suggest much bigger increases tied to U.S. tariffs are on the way.

----

Stocks Rise After Trump Unveils New Tariffs

U.S. stocks were mostly higher, putting the Dow on track to break a three-day losing streak after President Trump unveiled new tariffs on drugs, trucks and furniture.

----

Trump to Impose New Tariffs on Pharma, Big Trucks

Levies on drugs will be imposed on companies that aren't building plants in the U.S.

----

Consumer Sentiment Fell in September, According to University of Michigan Survey

The monthly survey's sentiment index fell to 55.1 in September, from 58.2 in August amid worries about high prices and the labor market.

----

Canada Economy Stalls in August After First Growth in Four Months

Canada's economy is on track to eke out tepid growth for the latest quarter, avoiding a second consecutive contraction.

----

Week Ahead for FX, Bonds: U.S. Jobs Data in Focus

Investors will hone in on U.S. jobs data, where any signs of weakness could reignite prospects of a series of interest-rate cuts in the months to come.

----

Fed's Barkin upbeat on U.S. economic outlook despite rising inflation, slower job growth

"The consumer is frankly exhausted," Tom Barkin said Friday.

----

Tokyo's Price Growth Steady But Rate Increase Still in Play

The pace of price growth in the Tokyo metropolitan area unexpectedly held steady in September, but upward pressure remains, keeping an interest-rate hike in play.

----

Europe's Right-Wing Populists Swerve Left on the Economy

The adoption of a high-spending, pro-welfare and protectionist agenda has been key in winning over blue-collar workers.

----

Fed Independence Reaches Its Moment of Truth as Supreme Court Weighs Cook's Fate

The court is poised to decide whether President Trump can remove Lisa Cook from the Federal Reserve's board, which former officials see as threat to central bank independence.

----

Democrats Dig In on Shutdown Stance After White House Threatens to Fire Workers

Republicans want a short-term deal to keep the government open, while Democratic leaders seek healthcare funds.

----

Bank of Mexico Makes 10th Straight Interest-Rate Cut

The board of governors voted 4-1 to lower the overnight interest-rate target to 7.50% from 7.75%.

data.title: Dow Jones Top Company Headlines at 1 PM ET: Boeing Clears Key Hurdle From 737 MAX Crashes | AstraZeneca ...
data.description:
Boeing Clears Key Hurdle From 737 MAX Crashes

The aerospace giant is regaining some authority from regulators to perform final safety checks on jets.

----

AstraZeneca to Offer Discounted Drugs As Trump Pressures Pharma Industry to Cut Prices

AstraZeneca said it will offer its asthma and diabetes drugs at an up to 70% discount in the U.S. ahead of a Trump administration deadline for pharmaceutical companies to cut drug prices.

----

Banks Ordered to Dig Through Account Closures to Find 'Debanking' Cases

Regulators demand information going back years as part of President Trump's crackdown on alleged political or religious discrimination.

----

Amgen to Invest $650 Million in U.S. Production Expansion as Trump Hikes Tariffs

The investment was announced a day after President Trump said he would impose a 100% tariff on pharmaceuticals produced by companies that are not building plants in the U.S.

----

Trump to Impose New Tariffs on Pharma, Big Trucks

Levies on drugs will be imposed on companies that aren't building plants in the U.S.

----

Meta to Launch Ad-Free Service on Facebook, Instagram in U.K.

The move matches an ad-free service launched in the EU in 2023, but hasn't been rolled out to the U.S. or other parts of the world.

----

U.S. Takes Aim at Chip Makers With New Plan to Throttle Imports

The administration wants domestic manufacturing to match imports and would impose tariffs on those companies that don't step up production.

----

Six Flags Is Under Activist Pressure to Sell Its Real Estate

Land & Buildings Investment Management has about a 2% stake in the theme-park operator.

----

How Zillow Got On Track for First Profitable Year Since 2012

The real-estate listings company has focused on boosting revenue and slowing head count growth to reach its profitability goal, CFO says.

----

GTCR to Buy Dentalcorp in Deal Valuing Dental Network at About $1.58 Billion

Dentalcorp said its shareholders will receive C$11 a share in cash, a nearly 33% premium to Thursday's closing price of C$8.28 for the Toronto company.

----

Brunello Cucinelli Shares Continue Slide Following Russia Claims

The company needs to start damage limitation as soon as possible to protect its reputation with customers and investors alike, Bernstein said.

----

Why Meta Thinks It Can Challenge Apple in Consumer AI Devices

Meta is betting that the future of artificial intelligence takes users way beyond smartphones and personal computers.

----

Intel Seeks Help From Apple and Others as U.S. Backs Comeback Bid

The semiconductor maker has been winning support from other tech companies following the deal that gave the U.S. government a 10% stake.

----

Nvidia's AI Deals Might Be 'Circular.' They're Also Smart Business.

Nvidia's cash pile is growing. It's using the money to backstop the future of AI.

data.title: Fitch Affirms Chile’s ‘A-’ Rating with Stable Outlook
data.description:
Fitch Ratings affirmed Chile’s long-term foreign currency rating at ‘A-’ with a Stable Outlook on September 26, 2025, citing strong governance and a solid debt repayment record.

The agency noted Chile’s ESG Relevance Score of ‘4[+]’ for Human Rights and Political Freedoms, supported by its above-50 percentile ranking in the World Bank’s Voice and Accountability indicator.

It also assigned a ‘4[+]’ score for Creditor Rights, reflecting more than two decades without a restructuring of public debt, which bolsters confidence in its repayment capacity.

Other ESG factors were rated ‘3,’ meaning they are credit-neutral with limited direct impact.

Fitch emphasized that ESG scores are not formal rating inputs but highlight the relevance of these factors to Chile’s credit profile.

data.title: Nigeria's Dangote refinery announces layoffs, cites acts of sabotage
data.description:
By Enes Tunagur and Isaac Anyaogu

Nigeria's Dangote oil refinery said on Friday it had laid off a small number of workers citing sabotage in various units, sparking criticism from an oil workers' union which said over 800 Nigerian workers were fired.

The workers were unjustly dismissed to be replaced by more than 2,000 Indian workers, the Petroleum and Natural Gas Senior Staff Association of Nigeria, known as PENGASSAN, said in a statement.

Africa's largest refinery, with crude processing capacity of 650,000 barrels per day, has created a new swing supplier in the Atlantic Basin with the potential to reshape global fuel trading.

"To clarify recent reports concerning the ongoing reorganisation within its facility ...This exercise is not arbitrary. It has become necessary to safeguard the refinery from repeated acts of sabotage that have raised safety concerns and affected operational efficiency," the refinery said in a statement.

It did not confirm how many workers had lost their jobs or whether the layoffs would affect production. More than 3,000 Nigerian staff continue to work for the plant, it said.

A copy of a letter from the refinery sent to one worker and seen by Reuters told the recipient they were fired with effect from the evening of Sept. 25.

Staff were laid off because they joined a union, an official at PENGASSAN said.

"This is totally wrong and we condemn it in its entirety," said Lumumba Okugbawa, secretary general of PENGASSAN.

The refinery, in its statement, said the company upholds internationally accepted labour principles including the right of every worker to freely decide whether or not to join a union.

Dangote shut its gasoline unit in late August for repairs likely to take 2-3 months.

The plant, which began processing crude in January 2024, exported a higher volume of fuel oil in September, according to shipping data firm Kpler.

Modern oil refineries typically export higher volumes of fuel oil when they have an outage or maintenance.

data.title: Microsoft's Grip on OpenAI Is Loosening. Why the Stock Remains a Buy. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Nate Wolf

Microsoft stock has stagnated in recent weeks with questions over its cloud-computing growth and its relationship with OpenAI weighing on shares. But the stock still has plenty of room to rise, according to Morgan Stanley.

Calling Microsoft a top pick, the firm reiterated an Overweight rating and lifted its price target on the stock to $625 from $582. "The durability of Microsoft's earnings growth and the company's premium return profile remains underpriced," wrote analyst Keith Weiss.

Shares ticked up 0.3% to $508.70 on Friday.

Concerns about the growing distance between Microsoft and its longtime partner OpenAI came to the fore earlier this month, when The Wall Street Journal reported that OpenAI had agreed to purchase $300 billion in computing power from Microsoft's cloud rival Oracle.

Given its right of first refusal in providing infrastructure to OpenAI, Microsoft appeared to decline the $300 billion offer, but that may be a positive sign for investors.

"Microsoft passing on the deal likely signals a high degree of comfort with the level of Enterprise demand they are seeing," Weiss said.

The company, like every AI cloud provider, has a limited amount of semiconductors and power. Allocating those resources to non-OpenAI clients means lower customer concentration, better profit margins, and greater long-term value, Morgan Stanley argued.

Microsoft shares have risen 1.6% since news of the Oracle deal broke on Sept. 10, in line with the S&P 500's 1.5% gain in that period, according to Dow Jones Market Data.

Another question hanging over Microsoft is whether the growth of its Azure public-cloud business is sustainable. Azure revenue grew by 39% year over year in the company's fiscal fourth quarter, ahead of analysts' estimates and an acceleration from 33% growth the quarter prior.

Despite the sky-high expectations, Microsoft remains in pole position to capture the value from enterprises migrating AI workloads to public clouds, Morgan Stanley said. And the company keeps spending to support that growth.

"Microsoft's level of AI-related capex over the last few years or our expectation in the coming years has not changed," Weiss wrote.

Despite the sluggish price movement in recent weeks, most analysts on Wall Street remain bullish on the stock. Of the 61 analysts polled by FactSet, 59 rate Microsoft a Buy or equivalent, with an average price target of just over $625.

Write to Nate Wolf at nate.wolf@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Energy companies gain after Russia cuts fuel exports
data.description:
** S&P 500 Energy index up 1.5%, tracking higher crude prices

** Brent crude up 1.2% at $70.31/barrel; U.S. West Texas Intermediate crude futures rise 1.1% to $66.14/barrel

** Crude prices rise after Ukraine's attacks on Russia's energy infrastructure prompted Moscow to curb fuel exports

** Energy majors Chevron and Exxon Mobil gain 2.1% and 1.8%, respectively

** Texas Pacific Land Corp , Schlumberger , Devon Energy and APA Corp rise between 3.2% and 4.5%, and are among the top percentage gainers on the energy index

** Oilfield services companies Baker Hughes up 1.1% and Halliburton up 2.8%

** Refiners Phillips 66 and Marathon Petroleum rise 1% and 1.7%, respectively

data.title: EU wheat eases as ample supply hangs over market
data.description:
European wheat futures edged lower on Friday in step with Chicago as a rebound from contract lows petered out in the face of abundant supply in exporting countries.

December wheat (BL2Z5) on Euronext's Paris-based futures market settled 0.7% down at 189.50 euros ($221.60) per metric ton.

The most-active contract on Euronext had regained some ground since reaching a contract low of 187.75 euros on Tuesday.

Chicago wheat also eased on Friday, pressured by weakness in corn. Grain market participants were looking ahead to U.S. government stocks estimates next week.

Wheat prices had found support mid-week from chart lows and signs of an upturn in import demand, with Algeria notably estimated to have booked around 600,000-700,000 tons of wheat in a tender on Tuesday.

However, latest crop forecasts underscored plentiful supply in exporting countries.

The European Commission on Thursday increased its forecast for European Union soft wheat production in 2025/26 to a 10-year high of 132.6 million metric tons, leading it to raise its projection of end-of-season stocks.

"We're tracking the fall in Chicago," a futures dealer said. "Fundamentally the market remains heavy."

Traders were also assessing the impact of a brief suspension this week of Argentina's grain export taxes that generated a burst of sales of wheat alongside other crops.

In France, a vessel is loading 65,000 tons of wheat for Egypt this week at the west coast ports of Nantes and Montoir, according to traders and shipping data.

That is in addition to a vessel due to load around 60,000 tons of wheat for Egypt at the northern French port of Dunkirk in the coming days, according to LSEG data. (GRAIN/SHP/FR)

However, traders are concerned that export demand appears light so far for the following months.

Showers and cool temperatures this week in France should help upcoming wheat planting, though the conditions may slow maize harvesting that is in its early stages, traders said.

($1 = 0.8551 euros)

data.title: KB Home's Build-to-Order Strategy Dependent on Strong Spring — Market Talk
data.description: KB Home said it wants to get back to its historic levels of building 70% of its homes to order rather than on spec. That's a worthwhile long-term goal, but its success will depend on strength of demand during the spring selling season, say Zelman & Associates analysts. Historically, the company has built a competitive advantage and higher margins through build-to-order homes. But right now, the company's backlog is down 22% year-over-year, which means it will need significant order growth in the first half of next year. KB Home also has less spec inventory to use as a buffer if demand for its build-to-order homes doesn't pick up.(nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1253 ET - KB Home said it wants to get back to its historic levels of building 70% of its homes to order rather than on spec. That's a worthwhile long-term goal, but its success will depend on strength of demand during the spring selling season, say Zelman & Associates analysts. Historically, the company has built a competitive advantage and higher margins through build-to-order homes. But right now, the company's backlog is down 22% year-over-year, which means it will need significant order growth in the first half of next year. KB Home also has less spec inventory to use as a buffer if demand for its build-to-order homes doesn't pick up.(nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1241 ET - Costco Wholesale's valuation doesn't match its slowing key performance indicators, Roth analyst Bill Kirk says. The retailer's same-store sales growth has been slowing down since it hiked membership fees, and U.S. growth hit a six-quarter low in 4Q. Membership renewal rates are also weakening. Kirk thinks Costco's performance will start to look more similar to conventional food retailers and will struggle to keep gaining as much market share as it has in recent years, as it gets more competition from Walmart and BJ's. "In our view, the valuation discrepancy between Costco and conventional peers is too wide," Kirk says. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1233 ET - Okta's customers appear increasingly interested in its identity governance offerings, D.A. Davidson analysts Rudy Kessinger and Andres Miranda Lopez say in a note after meeting with customers and channel partners at the company's annual user event. Customers shared modestly better feedback on Okta's identity governance and privileged access management products than the analysts observed in recent field work, they say. Okta's identity governance offerings are resonating particularly well with small- and mid-sized businesses, though larger enterprises are still predominantly using competitor SailPoint, they say.(kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1230 ET - American Airlines won't be able to stop the city of Chicago from awarding rival United Airlines five additional gates at O'Hare, a Cook County Circuit Court judge has ruled. Both airlines claim O'Hare a hub, but American has accused United of trying to squeeze it out and hampering American's expansion plans. It said Friday that the ruling would give United a competitive advantage in Chicago, but said it remains committed to the city. United President Brett Hart told employees that the decision was a "powerful validation of United's growth in the city. (alison.sider@wsj.com; @alyrose)

1212 ET - Nike faces high expectations as it approaches the back half of its fiscal year, limiting the possibility of further stock upside, Raymond James analysts say in a note. Though the company's revenue decline should moderate in its fiscal first quarter report, Wall Street already expects revenue growth to inflect positively in the back half of the fiscal year, starting in the fiscal third quarter, the analysts say. The magnitude of such growth will likely be limited given a high level of promotions and weak wholesale orders in China, among other headwinds, they say. "We don't have strong conviction on the magnitude of the positive inflection in F2H," they say. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1159 ET - Nike should show further progress in its turnaround strategy in its next earnings report, with potential for revenue to outperform prior guidance, Raymond James analysts say in a note. The analysts say they would not be surprised to see sales down low-single digits, versus the mid-single digit decline the company previously forecast. Nike's new innovation has been well received, and its wholesale and direct-to-consumer businesses are improving sequentially, the analysts say, though several headwinds remain, including rising costs and continued soft demand in China. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

0919 ET - Macroeconomic factors are leading Gen Z consumers to reduce their visits to fast-casual restaurants, causing a slowdown in sales for companies like Cava, Chipotle and Sweetgreen, says Benchmark analyst Todd Brooks. The federal government restarted student loan payment collections in May, while the unemployment rate for recent college graduates also grew to 5.8% in March of this year from 4.1% in March of 2023, according to the analyst. "We believe that these headwinds are leading younger consumers to cut their frequency of visiting their favorite fast casual concepts, as the price points for these visits can often run north of $20," Brooks says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0906 ET - Third quarter restaurant results may leave investors with a bad taste, says Benchmark analyst Todd Brooks. Placer AI data shows 43% of Benchmark's 30 covered restaurants tracking to miss consensus same-store sales by 150 basis points or more. Weaker sales among lower income consumers and in areas of heavy immigration enforcement are continuing and are being joined by more pressure on Gen Z consumers. "These pressures, combined with a broader discussion of mix headwinds on average checks from both incremental promotion and a slight increase in consumer check management all point to a dour 3Q release cycle for most," Brooks says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0857 ET - CarMax seems to be investing more on lowering prices but that hasn't led to a recovery in market share or same-store sales, which is a worrying sign, say JPMorgan analysts, who cut the stock's price target to $50 from $65. It suggests a slippery slope that could be hard to recover from and that pressures both gross profit per unit and volumes. Over the last five years, CarMax has made significant investments in omni-channel and sourcing functions but has been unable to gain share. "The business has not been agile enough to adjust to volatile industry backdrops despite prior experiences," the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0851 ET - Truck customers are likely to wait and see how new U.S. tariffs influence pricing, so expect orders and demand to take a hit in the near term, Jefferies analysts write. If prices increase across the industry in response, demand will likely be ht in the fourth quarter and into 2026, they add. Trump plans to impose 25% tariffs on heavy-duty trucks, but it isn't known how vehicles with parts made in the U.S., or which are USMCA-compliant, will be treated. Jefferies estimates that Daimler Truck and Traton could be at a 300-600 basis point cost disadvantage to manufacturers that assemble most vehicles in the U.S., such as Volvo and Paccar. Daimler Truck shares fall 2.3%, Traton shares are down 2% while Volvo shares rise 3.4%. Paccar shares rise 6.7% pre-market. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0850 ET - Switzerland plans to give UBS Group seven years to comply with tougher capital requirements, as the country's government detailed the timing of implementation of an update to its "too big to fail" regulatory regime. "The government points out that the 7-year period is depending on the parliamentary progress which may suggest that the end date is fixed," RBC Capital Markets says in a note. The Swiss Federal Council on Friday said a requirement to increase UBS's capital buffer will start in 2028 and be gradually implemented until the bank can fully back its foreign subsidiaries by the end of 2034. The government launched a consultation on the plan that is due to end on Jan. 9, 2026. UBS shares gain 0.2%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0840 ET - New U.S. drug tariffs came with an exception for companies building new factories in the U.S., but short-term levies could still be faced if projects aren't under way by Oct. 1, Panmure Liberum analysts write. Britain's GSK and AstraZeneca have announced major investments in the U.S., which might shield them, they write. However, those programs won't launch until 2026, a deadline that may expose the groups to short-term levies, the analysts say. Meanwhile, the announcement was encouraging for phrama groups that focus on generic drugs, as they appear to be exempt from tariff plans, they add. Hikma and Sandoz shares are up 1.7% and 1.3%, respectively, while GSK and AstraZeneca are steady. (william.gray@wsj.com)

data.title: Street View: Air Canada's updated outlook reflects flight attendant strike impact
data.description:
** Air Canada revised its 2025 adjusted EBITDA guidance to C$2.9 billion ($2.08 billion) to C$3.1 billion from C$3.2 billion to C$3.6 billion

** Shares fell 1.5% to C$17.7 during mid-day trading

** At least three brokerages cut PT

** Median PT of 15 analysts covering the stock is $25; average analyst rating is 'buy' - data compiled by LSEG

LABOUR DISRUPTIONS TOOK CENTRESTAGE

** CIBC ("Outperformer", PT: C$22) says the updated outlook reflects financial impact due to disruptions was softer and sees it as a positive surprise

** Stifel ("Buy", PT: C$24) says the labour disruptions had a substantial financial impact and tempers the airliner's buyback activity in November when the NCIB renews

** TD Cowen ("Buy", PT: C$22) expects the stock will not be volatile near-term until investors can gain clarity on the outlook for margins in 2026

($1 = 1.3943 Canadian dollars)

data.title: CEO.CA's Inside the Boardroom: Cascadia Gets 10-Year Exploration Permit, Starts 4,000m Diamond Drill Program at Carmacks Project
data.description:
Toronto, Ontario--(Newsfile Corp. - September 26, 2025) - CEO.CA ("CEO.CA"), the leading investor social network in junior resource and venture stocks, shares exclusive updates with CEOs of junior mining explorers.

Founded in 2012, CEO.CA, a wholly owned subsidiary of EarthLabs, Inc., is one of the most popular free financial websites and apps in Canada and for investors globally - with industry leading audience engagement and mobile functionality. Millions of people visit CEO.CA each year to connect with investors from around the world, share knowledge and view impactful stories about stocks, commodities, and emerging companies.

As a media partner at investor events around the world, CEO.CA provides coverage of the companies shaping the future of mining, meeting with industry leaders to learn more about their vision and strategy.

Meet the Executives Shaping the Mining Landscape

We caught up with Graham Downs, CEO of Cascadia Minerals Ltd. (OTCQB: CAMNF) to discuss what they gained after acquiring Granite Creek Copper, including 100% control of the road-accessible Carmacks copper-gold project. Downs also covers how their new 10-year exploration permit enables aggressive expansion goals of doubling the copper resource to over 1 billion pounds while targeting 500,000 to 1 million ounces of gold.

Cascadia Minerals Ltd


(OTCQB: CAMNF)

Cannot view this video? Visit:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5gOLsYETrmw

Tune into 'Inside the Boardroom' each week and be part of the conversation that's shaping the business landscape. Visit CEO.CA or our YouTube page for hundreds more executive interviews from CEO.CA here.

Interested in showcasing your company on 'Inside the Boardroom'? Get in touch with our team at sales@ceo.ca for further details and opportunities.

About CEO.CA

The leading community for investors & traders in junior resource & venture stocks. CEO.CA is one of the most popular free financial websites and apps in Canada and for small-cap investors globally — with industry leading audience engagement and mobile functionality. Since 2012, CEO.CA has brought millions of investors together from over 164 countries to discuss their portfolio holdings and find new investment opportunities. Download our App on iOS or Android marketplace or visit us today at CEO.CA to set up your free account.

CEO.CA is a wholly owned subsidiary of EarthLabs, Inc.

Highest price paid per share: GBp 3,171.0000

Broker: Barclays Capital Securities Limited ("Barclays")

The Company intends to cancel these Shares.

All shares were purchased from Barclays as an on exchange transaction subject to the rules of the London Stock Exchange.

Following the purchase and subsequent settlement and cancellation of these shares, the remaining number of ordinary shares in issue will be 807,658,996 (excluding treasury shares).

The figure of 807,658,996 may be used by shareholders (and others with notification obligations) as the denominator for the calculations by which they will determine if they are required to notify their interest in, or a change to their interest in, the Company under the Disclosure Guidance and Transparency Rules.

In accordance with Article 5(1)(b) of Regulation (EU) No 596/2014 (the Market Abuse Regulation), detailed information about the individual purchases is attached to this announcement.

data.title: Nvidia Falls After Record High As Investors Question AI Spending Spree
data.description:
Sep 26 - Nvidia shares slipped in Friday morning trading, down about 1.5% to $175, as investors continued to weigh the company's $100 billion investment commitment to OpenAI. The deal has attracted scrutiny because much of the money is expected to come back through chip purchases, raising questions about whether it reflects sustainable demand or circular financing.

The pullback comes just days after Nvidia set a record high of $184.55 on Monday. The stock briefly dipped below its 50-day moving average on Thursday before recovering to close higher, signaling mixed technical momentum. Even with the rebound, Nvidia has given back 3.6% from its early-week peak.

Peer chipmakers also saw weakness. Advanced Micro Devices fell 1.1% and Broadcom slipped 0.7% in early trading, showing that concerns extend beyond Nvidia. Micron Technology also moved lower alongside other AI-linked semiconductor names.

Analysts are constructive even in the face of pressure. Barclays maintained a Buy and a 240-price target on Nvidia whereas Mizuho Securities attributed the drop to a small reset, which pushed a group of investors to make profits following a robust September run.

data.title: Brazil coffee exports to US to fall further if tariffs stay, Cecafe president says
data.description:
Key points:
  • Cecafe reports further 20% drop in exports to US through Sept 19 after 46% slump in August
  • Trump-Lula UN meeting seen as positive for trade relations -Ferreira
  • Brazilian Conilon coffee exports to Colombia, Italy, UK rise as US demand falls

By Oliver Griffin

Brazil coffee exports to the United States will decline further if U.S. tariffs remain in place, Marcio Ferreira, the head of exporter group Cecafe said, adding the industry was pleased by warming relations between the leaders of the two countries.

U.S. President Donald Trump imposed a 50% tariff on Brazilian coffee and other goods, which came into effect in early August, amid tensions between his administration and the government of Brazilian President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva.

As a result, Cecafe reported that the U.S. was no longer Brazil's biggest market as coffee sales there fell 46% in August, compared with the year before, when Brazil saw record exports. Through September 19, exports to the U.S. were down a further 20% versus their level in August, Ferreira told Reuters on Thursday.

If tariffs continue, exports "will keep falling," said Ferreira, who also works as the superintendent of Tristao Trading, a leading exporter of Brazilian coffee.

Only a shift in policy could revive sales, he said, adding that the industry was encouraged by the positive exchange between Trump and Lula at the United Nations this week.

AN IRREPLACEABLE MARKET

U.S. tariffs on goods from Brazil, the world's largest coffee producer and exporter, have upended the global coffee market, pushing prices upwards.

In the short to medium term, the tariff restrictions may be good for producers, who have benefited from higher prices, Ferreira said, but added that exporters, roasters, and consumers are suffering.

Some producers are holding on to their stock, betting prices will return towards recent records, said Valdecir Schmidt, warehouse manager for Cooabriel, Brazil's biggest cooperative of Conilon, a type of coffee related to robusta.

"We have a very high stock level for this time of year," he said. "Last year, we didn't even have half of what we're seeing now, in the month of September."

As U.S. purchases shrink, exports to other countries are growing. According to Cecafe, total coffee exports to Colombia soared 578% in August.

Cooabriel's Conilon exports to Colombia - itself a major arabica coffee producer - increased 300%, said Jose Carlos Azevedo, the cooperative's sales manager, adding that places like Italy and Britain had also increased demand.

Still, the U.S. market is too big for other countries to replace it, Ferreira said. If tariffs remain in place for too long, Americans could grow used to other types of coffee, making it harder for Brazilian companies to recover their position in the U.S. market in the future, he added.

"It's time to get the kids out of the room and get the adults in to negotiate," he said.
